Call 2026 - Round 1
Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID: KA152-YOU-90046502 Deadline (Brussels Time): 12 Feb 2026 12:00:00
Project summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
In the contemporary digital landscape, where misinformation poses a serious threat, our project seeks to achieve different
objectives aimed at empowering youth to effectively navigate the complexities of the digital era. The primary goal is to
enhance the digital literacy and critical thinking skills of participants, providing them with the tools necessary to critically
evaluate information encountered in the digital realm. We believe it is important to distinguish between reliable and
misleading content, supporting a generation of digitally savvy individuals. Furthermore, our project aims to delve into the
intricacies of content marketing, manipulative algorithms and the role of social media in disseminating information.
Understanding these concepts is crucial for participants to grasp the mechanisms behind the spread of misinformation and
to develop a newer perspective on the digital landscape. The exploration of digital contracts, online scams and the evolution
of information dissemination represents another dimension of our project. Participants will gain insights into the implications
of digital contracts and privacy policies, while also learning to identify and counteract online scams. Understanding the
historical transition from traditional media to the dominance of social media platforms is integral to comprehending the
accelerated spread and impact of misinformation. A key focus of our project is to combat fake news, conspiracy theories
and information overload. Strategies will be developed to identify and counteract misinformation and participants will be
equipped to navigate the challenges posed by an overwhelming influx of information, which is crucial for decision-making
and mental well-being. Our project envisions participants not only as individuals with enhanced skills but also as responsible
digital citizens actively contributing to their local communities. The emphasis on boosting a sense of responsibility in the
digital space aligns with our overarching goal of creating a safe and inclusive environment. By instilling values of ethical
behaviour and responsible sharing, we aim to inspire participants to play an active role in countering misinformation.
Furthermore, "Misinformation in the digital area" project targets to deliver internationally participating young individuals
including people living with less opportunities together. The project will target international collaborations, cultural
awareness, breaking stereotypes and creating lasting friendships. Gender balance will produce a wider insight to several
subtopics. In essence, our project is driven by a recognition of the urgent need to address misinformation challenges.
Through the attainment of these objectives, we aspire to contribute to a broader societal impact by nurturing informed,
empowered and responsible youth capable of combating misinformation in the digital area.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
The key activities (also included in the timetable) planned for our project on combating misinformation in the digital era are
strategically designed to equip participants with critical skills and knowledge. In their implementation we plan to use non-
formal learning techniques as outlined. The central activities include: Digital Literacy and Critical Thinking Workshops:
Participants will engage in workshops to distinguish propaganda, fake news, conspiracy theories and enhance their critical
thinking and digital literacy, focusing on evaluating online information effectively. Social Media Watch and Responsibility
Sessions: We will explore the role of social media influencers, content creators and platforms, coupled with discussions on
responsible sharing practices to combat the dissemination of misinformation. We will have an emoji workshop where
participants will carefully review their use of the ever changing communication methods Discussion Panels on Content
Marketing and Algorithms:We will have fishbowl discussions that will delve into the role of algorithms, content marketing
strategies and their contributions to the spread of misinformation. Sessions on Information Overload, Online Ethics and
Online Scams: We will do a comprehensive session on the implications of digital ethics, privacy policies including GDPR
and online scams in the context of misinformation. Expert-Led Sessions on Critique on Scientific Studies: A workshop on
scientific studies will be done to provide a unique view on what scientific studies are to be believed .It will provide a diverse
and professional perspective in relation to their quality and their role in misinformation, disinformation and malinformation.
Interactive Exploration of Information Evolution: We will have discussions to investigate the historical transition from
traditional media to the dominance of social media platforms and its impact on the evolution of misinformation. Strategies to
Combat Fake News and Conspiracy Theories: We will have a session to develop strategies to identify and counteract fake
news and conspiracy theories, encouraging active participation and critical analysis. Creating a Safe and Inclusive
Environment/Online ethics: We will have a session where participants will talk about the social media ethics safe through
discussions on mutual respect, punctuality and active listening. Our activities will progress in the following order as will be in
the timetable attached: Commencing with icebreaker games on the arrival day, our program supports a welcoming
environment, adhering to the principle of inclusivity. The first day, following the introductory phase, is dedicated to
presenting the project, acquainting participants with the Youthpass and initiating discussions on the learning agreement. A
team-building activity serves as a unique approach to building connections, promoting friendships and understanding
individual strengths and weaknesses. To ensure the well-being of participants, we’ll have a Treasure hunt session in the
city. The next day will encompass sessions on business strategies to combat fake news and conspiracy theories,
emphasising active participation and critical analysis. Creating a safe and inclusive environment is a constant theme

throughout, with discussions on mutual respect, punctuality and active listening. The number of participants will be 40, 8
participants (18-30 years of age) including one group leader (min. 18 years of age), a total eight coming from each
countries. There will be two facilitators from Hungary to manage the project during the sessions and also help the people in
need. The participants for this project include young individuals with diverse backgrounds and fewer opportunities ranging
from students to young professionals, who have a genuine interest in media, technology, critical thinking and addressing
societal challenges. We will intentionally select participants from diverse European regions, including those from
disadvantaged or crisis-affected areas. This deliberate approach aims to bridge the gap by providing equal opportunities for
skill development and knowledge acquisition. Participation in the project will also be gender balanced to ensure
proportionality and equal representation. Participants are expected to be digital enthusiasts actively engaged with online
platforms and demonstrate motivation to combat misinformation. The project not only aims to equip participants with
essential skills but also establishes cross-cultural awareness through an international exchange format, creating an
environment where open-minded learners can collaboratively contribute to tackling the complexities of misinformation in the
digital age.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
In our mission to combat misinformation in the digital era, our project sets forth ambitious objectives designed to empower
participants with essential skills and insights. The core aim is to cultivate a robust understanding of critical thinking and
digital literacy, enabling participants to be thoroughly equipped to navigate the intricate landscape of online information.
Drawing inspiration from our past experiences in youth exchanges, we recognize the necessity for impactful and engaging
activities, ensuring that the program remains effective. Understanding the various aspects of the topic will empower
participants to make informed decisions and contribute to a culture of thoughtful information dissemination. As such,
participants will develop and gain an understanding of digital literacy skills. Participants will be better equipped with skills for
managing their content for example advertisements and sponsored content, as well as personal websites and blogs.
Organisations will experience increased visibility and promotional opportunities, attracting more and new interest in their
endeavours and activities. Consequently, they stand to gain new clientele from both the local community and international
collaborations, thereby supplementing their overall outreach and impact. Through dissemination of the project, partner
organisations will extend their reach to more young people, opening avenues for future opportunities and collaborations
hence developing their international presence for further projects. Which in turn promotes the topic, Erasmus+ as well as the
European Union values. In addition, a successful execution of the project is envisioned to strengthen the relationships
between partner organizations, supporting personal connections and ensuring efficient and transparent communication
channels. The knowledge gained during the youth exchange is anticipated to be valuable for the partner organizations
themselves as they will post the learning outcomes on SALTO for other NGOs to learn from the prepared material.
Participants through their personal conversations with other young people, social media engagement and presentations at
local events will get to be more active in the fight against misinformation. This engagement not only creates awareness but
also inspires entrepreneurship among peers, encouraging more young individuals to explore Erasmus+ initiatives. Local
communities, including parents and educators, benefit from the knowledge and skills disseminated by project participants.
Local businesses also gain hence financially improving the living standards of the locals. Besides friendship, building
networks is another significant outcome, which will create a stable connections between participating organisations and
even participants. This network may become the potential foundation for future collaborations and co-operations for other
projects addressing misinformation and promoting digital literacy.

Background and experience
Please briefly present your group. Please describe also how the group was created.
Our informal group is Youth Avenue, based in Budapest, Hungary. We are a vibrant group of young people, both local and
international students, who came together to promote unity, cultural understanding and personal growth. We create
opportunities for young people who would like to try and learn something new and gain some experience and knowledge for
personal and professional development. A critical priority for us revolves around cultivating an informed youth, as we firmly
acknowledge the strength of knowledge. Our objective is to facilitate the accessibility of information to the younger
generation, fostering their intellectual growth. Our informal group operates with the core values of the European Union,
including:
● Unity: We believe in the strength of diversity and the power of coming together to create positive change.
● Solidarity: We stand in solidarity with each other, supporting one another's growth and well-being.
● Equality: We advocate for equal opportunities and rights for all individuals, regardless of their background or identity.
● Inclusion: We strive to create an inclusive environment where everyone feels valued and respected.
At Youth Avenue, we organise a variety of local activities, including excursions and integration events, where members can
connect, explore Budapest and create lasting friendships. Additionally, we actively participate in Erasmus+ projects. We are
focusing on the themes of digitalization, access to information and fostering connections between Hungary, Europe and
Africa. That’s why - also in the close future - we will make a registered foundation from our informal group. We are already in
active talks with our Kenya and USA based partners. Through this cooperation we will be able to bring closer the cultural
relations between Hungary and Kenya. We plan it based on EU values matching small researches from our and partners’
members as well as active social media presence. At this stage (until we are not registered officially, we don’t have social
media just background work in progress).
Our initiatives are focused around youth development, aiming to empower young people to reach their full potential. We
place a strong emphasis on cultural awareness and education, providing opportunities for members to learn about different
cultures and traditions in the process breaking harmful stereotypes.
Does your group have any experience with youth work? if yes Please describe such experience.
We offer activities tailored to international mobilities, covering a wide range of topics related to youth development,
digitalization and cultural exchange. Whether it's through workshops, seminars, or collaborative projects, we strive to create
meaningful experiences that broaden perspectives and enrich lives.
Our local activities so far:
(cid:0) Dissemination activities locally for Erasmus+ funded projects.
(cid:0) Cooperation with Square of Youth Association and G.A.F.O.R.D. regarding international Erasmus+ mobilities as well as
for the Kenya based youth centre planning.
(cid:0) Board game nights by keeping members active through networking.
(cid:0) Excursions in Hungary to explore more places and the local culture.

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
Bokra Sawa, literally “Tomorrow Together” in Arabic, is a Euro Mediterranean association based in Marseille, with a
multicultural team composed of 6 active members.
Our main focus is the regional youth and we work on topics that concern them directly, through both a socio cultural and
artistic approach. Indeed, we believe that the youth is the first key resource we should invest in to observe a sustainable
evolution of our different societies. Therefore, our main field of action is the non-formal education to help young people
acquire essential skills,contributing to their professional and personal development, encouraging them to be active citizens,
and therefore, improving their employability, thus responding to their needs and interests.
Our aim is to offer new ways for Mediterranean and European youth to think about and discuss the world we live in, and to
equip them with the appropriate tools to express their ideas.
Bokra Sawa uses the creative tools of non formal education which include elements of art, culture and science with a focus
on specific actions such as exchange workshops, conferences, meetings or educational tools conception.
At the domestic level, we have worked in partnership with the city government, the central library and local schools and we
try to work closely with refugee’s communities in Marseille, specifically the youngsters who have fewer opportunities due to
their origins.
If mainly our projects have been developed in cooperation with Mediterranean countries, today, we have the aim to develop
our European network partners.

means for young people living on the shores of the Mediterranean or the Red sea to get to know each other, to unite through
common issues namely the protection of their environment, and ways to solve them and to exchange knowledge. This tool
has been designed for children aged between 11 to 17 years old and contains games and activities about the biodiversity of

Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous
experience that they will bring to the project.
The key staff involved in this project are people that have already wide experience in the field of learning mobility and that
have already participated and organized youth exchanges.
All the people in the Bokra Sawa’s team are able to communicate fluently in English and French languages and are
competent in non formal education. The staff is intercultural and multicultural, including people from different countries and
thus it’s trained to overcome cultural differences.
Catherine Ibrahim, General Coordinator of Bokra Sawa, with a long experience in the cultural field, holds a Master's Degree
in Communication from Cultural Organizations. She worked for nine years in the field of culture and Mediterranean
cooperation in Egypt, her origin country, and has settled since 2009 in Marseille. She benefits from a solid knowledge in the
cultural field in both societies and therefore, she is able to adapt to different cultural contexts. In 2013, she co-founded Bokra
Sawa association with a Euro Mediterranean team, in order to develop cultural cooperation projects between the two shores
of the Mediterranean region. Her responsibilities and tasks within the structure are to establish cultural projects within the
framework of the objectives of the association. She works on logistical issues, agreements between partners, grant
research and coordination between local and international partners. In this project she will be in charge of : - handling
communication with partners and team members ; - organizing dates for the preparation meeting - organizing visa issues ; -
handling budget monitoring and assess costs and rates ; - coordinating external agreements andcommunication with other
involved parties inthe project ; - coordinating the youth leader and partner’s organisation.
Our organisation works with the help of two Volunteers in Civic Service and some others volunteers European Voluntary
Service. These volunteers are guided by our General Coordinator and are responsible of the local management of the
project, including issues such as accommodation, transportation, local activities and field visits. These volunteers are
responsible for the animation of the activities,together with the facilitator. They are also responsible for the management of
the activities, in a pleasant environment and atmosphere for all participants, so that the quality of participation is maximized
and they are in adequation with the project objectives.
They also facilitate non-formal education activities that ensure inclusion of all participants in the project and they work on the
project communication, especially the translations of documents in foreign languages.

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
NGO SATELLITE is a non-governmental organisation dedicated to empowering and supporting young people aged 13-35
to actively participate in their communities, foster personal development, and contribute to positive social change. At first we
worked as a youth group, but after the war we decided to register as an NGO to more actively support young people in
harsh times. Our organisation works on the topics: youth advocating, moving rural youth forward, health and wellbeing,
entrepreneurship, project management and youth employment, Eco lifestyle and environmental protection, inclusion and
LGBTQIIA+ society support. We unite several youth centres all around Ukraine and support them through capacity building,
non-formal education training and access to international opportunities and good practices sharing. NGO SATELLITE is the
member of international network GYBN and coordinator of the national section of the JEF-Europe in Ukraine. The
organisation is the candidate for joining in the National Youth Council of Ukraine and Cooperation and Development network
of Eastern Europe.
What are the activities and experience of the partner organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your
organisation’s / group’s regular youth work activities at local level.
Speaking Club 'English for All" The Speaking Club is a community-driven initiative that aims to facilitate the development of
confident and practical communication skills among individuals. Through engaging in discussions on a range of topics - from
everyday anecdotes to more complex issues - members of the club can hone their speaking skills while enjoying the
process. One notable aspect of this club is its supportive and collaborative community, which provides an environment
conducive to learning and growth. Additionally, the club offers valuable networking opportunities for members to connect
with like-minded individuals and expand their professional or personal circles. By joining this club, individuals can engage
with youth from diverse regions and collaborate on everyday tasks and exercises while pursuing their academic goals. In
essence, the Speaking Club strikes the perfect balance between learning and fun, enabling members to enhance their
communication skills and enjoy the process of personal development.
Book Club "LiterNature" A book club is a community of readers who convene to discuss literature and exchange ideas. It
offers a unique opportunity to appreciate good reading and foster camaraderie by sharing insights, thoughts, and humour.
Our book club meets regularly online via Zoom once a month to explore selected books and engage in lively discussions.

In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected

Needs and objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
Under EU law, the ability to access and share information freely is recognized as a fundamental right. In addition, the United
Nations SDGs emphasize that the spread of information and communications technology and global interconnectedness
has great potential to accelerate human progress, bridge the digital divide and develop knowledge in societies. However,
alongside these vast opportunities, we face a critical challenge: the spread of misinformation. The issue of misinformation
has evolved into one of the most pressing challenges of the contemporary digital landscape, affecting politics, science,
health and social interactions. The 2023 Eurobarometer on Media and News found that 70% of Europeans are worried
about disinformation online, while in Hungary specifically, 65% of internet users report encountering fake news at least once
a week (European Commission, 2023). This is particularly alarming in a country where 92% of 16–24-year-olds use social
media daily (Eurostat, 2022). Hungarian examples highlight how misinformation impacts society. During the COVID-19
pandemic, false claims about vaccines circulated widely on Hungarian Facebook groups and Telegram channels,
contributing to vaccine hesitancy: surveys in 2021 showed that only 45% of Hungarians trusted vaccines initially, one of the
lowest rates in the EU (ECDC, 2021). Another example is the 2022 parliamentary elections, where fact-checking platforms
like Lakmusz.hu documented dozens of viral disinformation cases targeting young voters. These cases illustrate the growing
vulnerability of Hungarian youth to emotionally charged, misleading content. Young people are particularly at risk. As “digital
natives,” they are active users of platforms like TikTok, Instagram and Facebook but often lack the critical thinking skills to
navigate this information landscape. A 2022 OECD report showed that only 44% of Hungarian 15-year-olds could
distinguish between fact and opinion online, which is below the EU average. This lack of digital literacy makes them more
vulnerable to misinformation, disinformation and malinformation, as well as to the emotional manipulation these can
provoke. Misinformation fuels divisive narratives, deepens social divides and increases inequalities by targeting vulnerable
groups. It has been linked to hate speech, online harassment as well as real-world violence. In Hungary, NGOs such as
Political Capital have warned that conspiracy theories about migrants or LGBTQ+ people spread online have contributed to
higher levels of polarization and intolerance among youth. The risks are not only political: algorithms on platforms like
TikTok and Facebook often prioritize sensational, emotionally charged content over balanced reporting, amplifying biased
or false narratives. Consequently, with the growing influence of social media influencers and content creators, the line
between fact and fiction has blurred. In Hungary, e.g., some popular YouTubers have been criticized for spreading
unverified claims about climate change or mental health, shaping the opinions of thousands of young followers. The ease of
going viral also creates pressure on youth to share content quickly, without verifying its accuracy, which normalizes
irresponsible digital practices. Another dimension of this challenge is digital contracts and online privacy. In Hungary,
research from the Nemzeti Média- és Hírközlési Hatóság (NMHH, 2022) found that over 70% of young internet users accept
cookies or terms of service without reading them, leaving them vulnerable to scams and unethical data practices. In
addition, the normalization of adult content online has psycho-social effects on youth, including distorted perceptions of
relationships and increased exposure to exploitation. Recognizing these challenges, our youth exchange project is not just
about raising awareness—it is about empowering young people to become informed and responsible digital citizens.
Addressing misinformation requires more than passive consumption of facts; it demands active engagement, critical
analysis as well as ethical responsibility in online spaces. Our project offers young people the opportunity to develop these
essential skills through experiential learning, real-life case studies and interactive activities. By analyzing Hungarian and
European case studies (e.g., COVID-19, elections, climate denial campaigns), participants will learn to question, investigate
and verify the content they encounter. We are conducting this youth exchange because young people are at the heart of
societal change and their capacity to shape the future is immense. By equipping them with tools to critically evaluate
information, understand the mechanics behind online content or recognize the ethical implications of digital behavior, we aim
to contribute to a generation that is resilient against the dangers of misinformation.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
Misinformation in the Digital Era’s objectives strongly align with the Erasmus programme. First and foremost, this project
aims to promote critical thinking through a non-biased approach and an in-depth exploration of the challenges posed by
misinformation, disinformation and malinformation. In addition, inclusion, equity and the concept of mobility are integrated
into our project. We also explore how misinformation has led to misunderstandings on sustainability, climate change and
environmentally friendly practices. Our project’s major theme is digital literacy and ethical responsibility, particularly
concerning the consumption and sharing of information. Misinformation often targets marginalized communities and
perpetuates harmful stereotypes and through our project, we aim to raise awareness of these issues and encourage
responsible sharing that supports solidarity and respect for diversity. Through this project, participants will delve into how
digital misinformation affects societal trust and decision- making, exploring strategies to combat false information. By
promoting awareness of how algorithms, content marketing and digital contracts contribute to misinformation, the project

knowledge, skills and attitudes/behaviours) are to be acquired/improved by participants in the activity?
Participants are anticipated to gain valuable insights into the different dimensions of misinformation in the digital era through
the interactive sessions and activities outlined in the timetable.
Knowledge: Information literacy: They will learn to critically assess information sources by practicing with real case studies
(e.g. COVID- 19 vaccine disinformation in Hungary, election-related fake news from 2022). This includes distinguishing
between reliable and misleading content, recognizing “clickbait” headlines, and identifying manipulated images or videos.
Media literacy: They will understand the mechanisms of misinformation across media formats — written articles, social
media posts, memes, short-form videos — by analyzing how algorithms on TikTok, Instagram, and Facebook amplify
sensational content. Digital literacy: They will explore how to navigate online spaces safely and responsibly, including
recognizing phishing attempts, understanding privacy settings, and learning to protect personal data. For example,
participants will review the Hungarian Media Authority’s (NMHH) 2022 research showing that 70% of young people accept
cookies without reading terms, and they will discuss safer alternatives. Sustainability link: They will investigate the
environmental impact of misinformation, e.g., how “climate change denial” campaigns spread online, and how fake
sustainability claims (greenwashing) mislead consumers. This connects environmental issues with digital narratives and
promotes eco-friendly choices. Health and well-being: They will learn about the psychological effects of misinformation, such
as anxiety, panic, or polarization. Participants will practice mindfulness strategies for responsible media consumption and
reflect on how disinformation during COVID-19 affected mental health in Hungary and Europe.
Skills: Critical thinking: By engaging in debates, role plays, and “fact-checking labs,” they will practice questioning sources,
spotting logical inconsistencies, and analyzing arguments. Communication: Through structured debates and group
presentations, participants will refine their ability to express ideas clearly, counter misinformation respectfully, and adapt
messages for different audiences (e.g. peers vs. parents). English and intercultural competence: Since English will be the
working language, participants will naturally improve their language skills. In addition, through intercultural workshops and
culture nights, they will explore how misinformation varies across countries (e.g. stereotypes about migrants in Hungary vs.
Spain), developing empathy and global awareness. Collaboration and teamwork: Non-formal methods (problem-solving
tasks, simulation games, small-group challenges) will strengthen cooperation, negotiation, critical thinking, and collective
decision-making. Civic competence: Through case studies (e.g. how misinformation influenced Hungary’s election
campaigns or attitudes toward minorities), participants will understand the societal consequences of disinformation and
reflect on their own responsibility in democratic participation. Entrepreneurial skills: Participants will be challenged to design
small-scale campaigns or creative tools (e.g. a TikTok video series, infographic, or podcast idea) to counter misinformation.
This supports innovation, leadership, and project management skills. Attitudes/Behaviours: Responsible digital citizens:
They will develop the habit of verifying content before sharing, becoming more cautious about spreading unverified news.
Ethical awareness: They will become more aware of online privacy, consent, and the dangers of manipulation, choosing
responsible online behaviours. Cultural openness: By exploring stereotypes and misinformation across cultures, they will
cultivate empathy and respect for diversity. Resilience: By understanding how misinformation spreads and manipulates
emotions, participants will become more resistant to polarization, hate speech, and conspiracy theories. Concrete outputs of
learning: A set of youth-created digital guidelines (“10 Tips for Spotting Fake News”) in multiple languages. Mini campaigns
(videos, graphics, blog posts) created during workshops and shared on partner organisations’ social media. Personal action
plans where each participant outlines how they will apply new skills in their local communities (e.g. school presentations,
NGO volunteering or peer education).

The activities, also included in the timetable, will encompass engaging workshops in the topics of fake news, role of
misinformation on sustainability & climate change, propaganda, stereotypes and will employ participatory methods like
challenges to identify deep fakes, false information together with presentations encouraging active involvement and
boosting critical thinking. Workshops will explore topics such as media literacy, information verification and digital literacy.
We will also use interactive simulations to recreate real-life scenarios related to misinformation, allowing participants to
navigate challenges and develop practical skills in identifying and countering false information. The "Viral Impact Simulation"
activity will immerse participants in a controlled environment where they create and share content to observe the dynamics
of going viral. We aim for our participants to learn about environmentally friendly practices and the role misinformation
across the internet and social media has had to play in perpetuating narratives such as climate change is a hoax. Through
this we hope to change the attitudes of not only our participants but also the stakeholders and general public. Through
reflections, group discussions and case studies, participants gain insights into the positive and negative consequences of
content going viral, boosting digital competence, media literacy and responsible communication skills. In addition, we will
have structured debates and open discussions on ethical social media practices to encourage participants to express
diverse viewpoints, promoting dialogue and the exchange of ideas. The aim is to enhance communication skills and promote
a deeper understanding of the complexities of misinformation. Participants will also engage in role-playing exercises during
the stereotypes activity to help debunk and understand how certain narratives have been spread to enhance intercultural
competence. A cultural night organised by participants will serve as an opportunity to share cultural experiences and explore
the role of culture in shaping perceptions. The venue of the activities will be in Eger, Hungary, that features workshop
facilities and an open area for the outdoor program components. The outdoor setting provides opportunities for team-
building activities and nature-inspired discussions. The venue is equipped with essential technical support i.e. projectors
and Wi-Fi to ensure smooth implementation of digital learning methods. Access to reliable internet connectivity will enable

Facebook groups will be created to serve as primary channels for day-to-day communication, quick updates and

Project design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
In anticipation of the youth exchange project on "Misinformation in the digital area," we have outlined a comprehensive plan
to ensure the well-being and effective engagement of participants throughout the entire project lifecycle.
Before the Activity: Prior to departure, participants will establish connections through social media platforms like Facebook
or WhatsApp, facilitated by our partners during pre-departure meetings. These meetings will serve as a platform to deliver
critical information about the project's objectives, participants' roles and the potential benefits they can derive. An
information pack will be distributed to provide detailed insights into project activities, objectives, goals and practical
information about the hosting country, covering needed aspects such as costs, travel, insurance, visa, accommodation and
security. To enhance the preparatory phase, workshops on intercultural communication and linguistic basics will be
conducted to establish cultural sensitivity and effective communication among participants. Additionally, comprehensive
risk-prevention training will ensure participants are well-versed in recognizing and mitigating risks associated with online
activities, including a thorough review of insurance coverage. Attention to dietary concerns, preferences and allergies will
create an inclusive and safe environment. The organizers will also also observe the possibly occurring risks. We will prepare
for bad weather, emergencies etc. and will communicate it with the partners accordingly. We will also ask our partners to
communicate and prepare their participants for the exchange based on the provided infopack as well as useful information
that involves risk prevention: emergency phone numbers, medical accessibility and need for insurance as well as all the
necessary informationa about the country and the venue (including the transport details and national laws). Meeting with the
team leaders online will be crucial from risk prevention perspective as well as gathering and sharing needed information.
The online meeting with the team leaders and their invited participants as well as the national team online preparations will
be establishing basics for common understanding. This will help the work of the facilitators during implementation, as well as
the participants' approaches and expectations towards the project and the experience overall. Partners will provide
information about special needs and support the organizers to integrate the people in need based on equal participation.
During the Activity: Throughout the project, daily reflection sessions will be included and guided to provide participants with
a platform for processing learning experiences and sharing insights. Mentorship and support will be a key focus, with
assigned buddies for personalised guidance and regular check-ins to address concerns. Diverse learning formats, such as
workshops, group projects and interactive sessions, will cater to varied learning styles. Team leaders will play a key role for
translations also.
After the Activity: Post-activity workshops will be organised to review and support learning, discuss post-activity
experiences and explore practical strategies for applying acquired skills in participants' daily lives. A digital resource hub will
be created, offering participants access to project materials, additional readings and relevant resources for continued
learning. Furthermore, an alumni network will be established, fostering continued connection and collaboration among past
participants. This network will provide opportunities for alumni to contribute to future projects, share experiences and
actively participate in ongoing initiatives. As the participants will help to disseminate the project results through the outputs
and final outcomes of the project that they prepared, they can organise local dissemination activities physically with their
peers and local communities, so the experience and the topic will be shared with others. The national team’s team leaders
and local organisations and informal groups will support them in their implementation and reporting process of each activity
that they organise including the online content sharing. This integrated approach aims to not only prepare participants for
the project but also support them comprehensively before, during and after the activity.
Team leaders will have a key role not just in communication related matters but also as risk prevention within their national
teams especially during the implementation phase. Before that they will support with the proper preparation the participants,
so they can be ready and fully active during the exchange, also their learning experience and goals can meet. The hosting
and partner organizations will rely on the team leaders in several matters as the representatives of the organizations and
their participants. They will maintain active communication and raise any kind of matter to the organizers.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
Ensuring the safety and well-being of participants is a top priority and we have developed a comprehensive security plan
that covers all stages of the project. With the support of our partner organizations, we will implement strict safety protocols
before, during, and after the exchange to create a secure and inclusive fulfilling experience for all.
Before departure, all participants will attend a mandatory pre-departure workshop, where they will receive detailed safety
briefings on emergency protocols, cultural adaptation and responsible behavior from their sending organizations. The
workshop will cover risk awareness, local emergency services (police, hospitals, embassies) and travel safety. Team
leaders will conduct individual check-ins with participants to assess any medical, psychological or security concerns. A
confidential health questionnaire will be collected to identify medical conditions, allergies or specific needs, ensuring
appropriate support during the exchange. A comprehensive risk assessment will be conducted in collaboration with local
partners before participants’ arrival, identifying safety challenges and mitigation measures.
Upon arrival, participants will be formally introduced to safety guidelines, emergency contacts and designated safe zones.
The accommodation will meet high security and hygiene standards, featuring lockable rooms, controlled access and
proximity to medical facilities. Participants will be housed in gender-appropriate rooms, with at least two per room to ensure
support in case of emergencies. Facilitators will stay nearby to provide on-site assistance if needed. Health precautions are

a priority. All participants must have valid medical insurance, such as the European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) or
equivalent. First aid kits will be available at all activity sites and trained first aid responders will be designated among
facilitators and team leaders. Local healthcare providers, pharmacies and hospitals will be identified before the exchange
and participants will receive guidance on how to access medical assistance. To ensure a safe, respectful and inclusive
environment, all participants will adhere to a strict code of conduct, which will be shared before arrival. The code will outline
expectations regarding behavior, respect for cultural diversity and inclusion. It will prohibit harassment, discrimination,
bullying, drug and alcohol abuse, and any inappropriate conduct. A zero-tolerance policy will be enforced and participants
will be encouraged to report concerns confidentially. Designated safeguarding officers will be available to handle complaints
sensitively and efficiently. Emergency preparedness is fully integrated into the project. A detailed emergency response plan
will cover scenarios such as medical emergencies, missing participants, fire evacuation and natural disasters. Regular
safety briefings and emergency drills will reinforce participant preparedness. The project team will maintain direct
communication with local authorities, including police, embassies and health services, ensuring a quick response to critical
situations. Participants will always be encouraged to travel in groups when outside project activities, following the “safety in
numbers” principle. Guidelines on safe areas, transportation and emergency contacts will be provided. Team leaders will
receive emergency contact lists for all participants and will be responsible for communicating protocols in case of accidents,
lost documents or unexpected incidents. Special attention will be given to participants with fewer opportunities, ensuring
their specific needs are anticipated and met. Organizers will work closely with them to address any accessibility concerns,
mental health considerations or personal safety requirements, ensuring their full inclusion and participation. A post-
exchange follow-up will be conducted to check in on participants’ well-being after their return. Feedback will be collected on
safety measures, allowing for continuous improvement in future projects. Lessons learned will be shared with partner
organizations, reinforcing best practices in youth mobility programs. Through meticulous preparation, real-time monitoring,
and active collaboration with local stakeholders, we are committed to providing a safe and supportive environment where
participants can fully engage in the learning experience without safety concerns. This comprehensive approach ensures that
every participant feels secure and focused on maximizing their personal and professional growth during the exchange. Data
protection and GDPR compliance: Given the strong digital and online component, data protection is a priority. All virtual
tools and platforms will be used in line with EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) standards. Consent forms:
Participants will sign consent forms covering data usage, photos, videos and social media appearances.
What activities are foreseen after the end of the Youth Exchange? How will the participants follow-up on the activity?
Following the culmination of the Youth Exchange project on misinformation in the digital area, an array of activities is
anticipated to extend the impact beyond the exchange dates. Participants will engage in reflection workshops, virtually.
These sessions will be intended to give them the chance to share their experiences, reflect on personal growth and discuss
what they learned during the project in relation to the themes but not limited to misinformation and fake news or propaganda.
They will also take part in a formal evaluation of the Youth Exchange by an anonymous form, providing feedback on their
experiences and sharing suggestions for improvement. This evaluation will help organizers refine their future projects and
ensure continuous improvement in program delivery. The form will include what they learned, shortcomings, overall
satisfaction, improvement areas, critique, facilitator professionalism, partner countries etc. A comprehensive final report will
be compiled, detailing project achievements, educational results, testimonials and recommendations for future actions. This
report will serve as a valuable resource, providing insights into the project's impact and serving as a guide for future
initiatives. Participants will actively share their experiences through diverse mediums, such as social media posts, personal
discussions, videos and images. Leveraging websites and social media platforms, partner organizations will disseminate
project-related content, allowing participants to upload project photos and craft short reports. The use of hashtags, like
#Erasmus+,#Misinformation will be promoted to track and amplify participants' posts, fostering increased visibility and
engagement. We hope in regards to misinformation, that our participants become independent fact checkers and promote
truth, honesty and a sense of responsibility in their virtual activities. A cloud-based storage system will be established to
collect non-formal education methods, activity results, photos and relevant materials, ensuring accessibility for all
participants and participating organizations. Furthermore, project outcomes will be showcased on the Erasmus+ Projects
Results Platform and on SALTO, contributing to the official database. Additionally, partner organizations will organise info
sessions where participants can share their experiences, learnings and present the enterprises developed during the
project. These events will be open to the community, aiming to inform young people about EU opportunities and inspire them
to engage in similar projects. The participants will disseminate their experience and knowledge through their own and their
home organisation’s social media platform. The final outcomes will be translated to more languages and will be available to
download for further actions for free of charge. The final outcomes will be also posted on SALTO as a handbook of good
practices that will be prepared by the participants chapter by chapter with the contents of each session. We anticipate the
project to ignite a passion for careers in the Information Technology industry, sustainable development for some
participants. We will endeavour to provide professional mentorship by providing them with valuable skills in intercultural
communication, misinformation detecting practices and project management. Participants will be encouraged to continue
their involvement in the Erasmus+ program by either participating in or organizing new youth exchanges, training courses or
volunteering projects that further explore the topic. The dissemination efforts will extend to target a broader audience,
especially those unfamiliar with the possibilities offered by programs like Erasmus+ or ESC. The goal is to amplify the voices
of active citizens, drawing attention to the significance of youth participation in combating misinformation in the digital area.

Recognition of learning outcomes
How will you support participants to be aware of what they have learned and which competences they have developed or
improved? Please remember to include the methods that support reflection and documentation of the learning outcomes in
the daily timetable of each activity.
Participants engage in discussions about their expectations, the project's aims and the broader context of Erasmus+. Early
introduction to the mission of the Erasmus+ program, the Youthpass framework and key competences helps participants
understand the practical value of their learning experience and how they can track their personal and professional
development throughout the exchange. This structured approach ensures that participants do not merely engage in the
activities but actively reflect on their growth, skill development and the competences they acquire during the mobility. To
reinforce the learning process from the very beginning, the Youthpass framework will be introduced not as a mere
certification tool but as an essential instrument for recognizing non-formal learning. Participants will be guided in self-
assessment from day one to help them acknowledge their strengths, areas for improvement and progress throughout the
exchange. To support this, facilitators will organize interactive introduction sessions where participants set personal learning
goals based on the key competences outlined in Youthpass. These learning goals will serve as reference points for self-
reflection and group evaluations throughout the exchange. A key component of the learning process is continuous
documentation and reflection. To make learning tangible and relatable, participants will be encouraged to use creative and
engaging methods to document their progress daily. They will have the opportunity to create digital diaries, maintain video
logs or write blog posts to articulate their experiences, lessons learned and key takeaways from the sessions. Additionally,
social media engagement will be leveraged as an interactive tool for reflection, where participants can post insights, creative
content, and photos that encapsulate the core messages of each activity. This not only serves as a personal record of their
learning journey but also contributes to the broader dissemination of project results. Reflection sessions will be
systematically integrated into the daily schedule to ensure that learning is actively recognized and internalized. Each day will
conclude with structured reflection activities, facilitated through small group discussions, guided journaling and thematic
storytelling exercises where participants share personal insights from the day’s activities. These sessions will not only allow
participants to consolidate their understanding but also enable facilitators to track learning trends and make real-time
adjustments to better meet participants' needs. In addition to individual reflections, peer feedback will play a crucial role in
recognizing learning achievements. Participants will engage in collaborative activities such as mind mapping, mood board
creation that visually represent their understanding of key topics. Regular peer-to-peer evaluations will provide constructive
feedback on group projects, enhancing participants’ ability to recognize their strengths, receive insights from others and
refine their ideas. Halfway through the mobility, a structured mid-term evaluation will be conducted to assess participants’
progress toward their learning goals. This checkpoint will allow them to reflect on their experiences, reassess their learning
objectives and make any necessary adjustments to maximize their development. Facilitators will also employ experiential
learning techniques that challenge participants to apply their knowledge in real- world contexts. Workshops will be followed
by hands-on implementation exercises where participants will put theoretical concepts into practice. Case studies,
simulations, and role-playing activities will allow them to engage with complex issues dynamically, reinforcing key skills such
as critical thinking, problem-solving as well as teamwork. As the project progresses, reflection will become deeper and more
analytical. Towards the conclusion of the exchange, a final structured reflection session will encourage participants to
synthesize their learning experiences into actionable insights they can carry forward beyond the exchange. This process will
culminate in the completion of their Youthpass certificates, where they will detail the competences they have gained and
how these skills can be applied in their personal, academic and professional lives. The distribution of Youthpass certificates
will be more than just a formal closing activity; it will be a moment of recognition and celebration. Each participant’s journey,
growth, and contributions will be acknowledged, reinforcing their achievements and boosting their confidence in applying
these newly acquired competences in future endeavors. To ensure long-term impact, participants will be encouraged to
share their learning outcomes in their local communities through presentations, blog articles or small projects that raise
awareness of digital literacy and misinformation.

How will you reach out to these participants?
To reach young people with fewer opportunities, we will apply a targeted outreach strategy based on direct contact, existing
trust relationships and local youth work structures. We will not rely solely on open public calls, as these often fail to reach
young people facing structural barriers. Instead, outreach will be proactive, personalised and carried out through
organisations and actors who already work with the identified target groups. Each partner organisation will be responsible
for identifying and reaching potential participants through their existing local networks. These include youth centres, informal
youth groups, schools with higher drop-out rates, community organisations and social youth services. Outreach will be
embedded into regular youth work activities, where young people already feel safe and included. For barriers linked to
discrimination, outreach will be done through trusted intermediaries such as youth workers, mentors and peer leaders who
already work with young people exposed to ethnic, gender-based, social or identity-based discrimination. Instead of mass
promotion, these young people will be approached individually or in small groups. The project will be presented as an
opportunity for learning and personal growth, without emphasising “disadvantage” or labelling participants. This approach
reduces fear of stigmatisation and increases willingness to apply. To reach young people facing barriers linked to education
and training systems, partner organisations will cooperate with alternative education providers, second-chance education
programmes and youth NGOs. Information about the project will be shared during non-formal sessions, workshops or
mentoring meetings. The project will be explained in clear, simple language, highlighting that no formal qualifications,
academic background or previous Erasmus+ experience are required. For cultural differences, outreach will rely strongly on
peer-to-peer communication. Former participants and volunteers or group leaders with intercultural backgrounds will share
their own experiences informally, both online and offline. This includes short presentations during youth activities and small
group discussions. Using peers as messengers helps reduce fear related to language use, intercultural environments. In the
case of economic barriers, outreach materials and verbal communication will explicitly clarify that participation is free of
charge. This information will be communicated early and clearly, as financial concerns are a common reason for self-
exclusion. Partner organisations will directly approach young people known to face financial hardship and explain the
funding structure in a transparent way, so that economic assumptions do not prevent them from applying. To address
geographical barriers, especially for young people living in rural or remote areas, outreach will combine local contacts and
digital tools. Local youth workers, teachers and community leaders will share information in person where possible. At the
same time, social media posts and direct messages will be used to reach young people who have limited access to physical
youth spaces. This ensures equal access to information regardless of location. For social barriers, such as isolation, low
self-confidence or weak social networks, outreach will be based on personal encouragement rather than competitive
selection. Youth workers will actively recommend the project to individuals who might not consider themselves “suitable” for
international projects. The application process will be explained step by step and young people will be encouraged to ask
questions before applying. Our outreach strategy is intentional, local as well as relationship-based. By working through
trusted youth work structures and adapting communication methods to different barriers, we ensure that young people with
fewer opportunities are genuinely reached and informed about the youth exchange, rather than expected to find the
opportunity on their own.
